_meta.description: Cool facts per species for use in Story Strip trivia, Silence mode, and Species Room side-content.
_meta.source: Hand-curated from natural history references. Short, punchy, <25 words each. Intended for casual reading.
_meta.format: One array of strings per species. Rotation-friendly.

Mourning Dove: They can drink by suction — one of the very few birds that don't need to tip their head back.
Parents feed chicks 'crop milk,' a curdled secretion from the adult's esophagus lining.
That mournful cooing you mistake for an owl is how they got their name.
Their whistling takeoff is made by their wings, not their voice.
They mate for life and can raise up to six broods per year in the south.
One bird can store up to 17,200 bluegrass seeds in its crop before digesting.
They pick grit constantly — up to 50 pebbles a day to grind their food.
Despite being hunted heavily, populations hold steady above 400 million.
They fly with explosive bursts up to 55 mph to escape predators.
Indigenous Mesoamerican peoples considered them messengers between the living and the dead.
House Finch: Eastern populations descend from a few dozen birds released in New York in 1940 by pet dealers.
A male's red color comes entirely from his diet — poorly fed males are yellow or orange.
Females prefer the reddest males: bright red signals he's a good forager.
They readily nest in hanging flower baskets — a behavior unknown in their native western range.
Their song has regional dialects, like human accents, shaped by who they grow up around.
They were the first wild songbird documented catching a domestic poultry disease.
They can drink salt water, a rare trait among songbirds.
A single feeder can host over 1,000 House Finch visits per winter day.
Their scientific name translates roughly to 'Mexican red-blood.'
Flocks travel in tight formations with constant chirping contact calls.
Song Sparrow: Every male has a unique repertoire of 5–20 songs — essentially a personal signature.
Males learn their songs by listening to neighbors, not their fathers.
There are over 30 recognized subspecies across North America — enormous variation in one species.
They sing year-round, even in deep winter, unlike most songbirds.
A male can sing over 2,000 songs in a single day during territory disputes.
They prefer to nest on the ground despite the predation risk.
The 'song' in Song Sparrow is official — they were named for how much they sing.
They're one of the most widely distributed songbirds in the Americas.
Pacific coast Song Sparrows are dark and large; desert ones are pale and small.
They were the subject of the first modern scientific study of a wild songbird (1930s).
Black-capped Chickadee: They grow new neurons every fall to remember thousands of seed cache locations.
A single bird can stash seeds in thousands of different spots and recover them months later.
Their classic 'chick-a-dee' call has meaning — more 'dees' signals more danger.
Other species eavesdrop on chickadee alarm calls to know when hawks are around.
They can drop their body temperature 20°F overnight to survive winter.
Each chickadee's song has a distinct two-note 'hey-sweetie' pitch it can transpose at will.
Males aggressively defend a 2-acre territory but join mixed flocks outside breeding season.
They're the state or provincial bird of Massachusetts, Maine, and New Brunswick.
Despite being tiny, they outcompete larger birds for cached food through sheer memory.
They form hierarchies within flocks — top birds eat first and sleep in the warmest spots.
Dark-eyed Junco: They're sometimes called 'snowbirds' because they appear at feeders with the first snow.
There are six distinct color morphs once classified as separate species.
Most eastern Juncos breed in Canadian boreal forests and only visit us in winter.
They prefer to forage on the ground — you'll almost never see them at a hanging feeder.
White outer tail feathers flash during flight as a signal to flock-mates.
They form strict winter pecking orders — subordinate birds eat leftovers.
Their spring migration is triggered by day length, not temperature.
A flock can contain 15–30 birds, often returning to the same yard year after year.
They're one of the most abundant forest birds in North America — 630 million strong.
Males arrive on breeding grounds two weeks before females to claim territory.
Northern Cardinal: They don't migrate — the same birds live in your yard year-round for their whole lives.
Males and females both sing, unusual among North American songbirds.
Their red pigment comes from carotenoids in berries and seeds they eat.
They're the state bird of seven US states, more than any other species.
Pairs often duet, with the female's song subtly different from the male's.
A male will feed his mate beak-to-beak during courtship — looks like kissing.
Some cardinals are yellow instead of red due to a rare genetic mutation that blocks red pigments.
They can live over 15 years in the wild — remarkably long for a songbird their size.
Their crest raises when they're excited or agitated, like a mood indicator.
They were named for Catholic cardinals' scarlet robes, not the other way around.
Tufted Titmouse: They hoard seeds one at a time, flying back and forth to a cache tree — often the same tree for years.
They pull fur from living mammals — including sleeping dogs and humans — to line their nests.
Despite tiny size, their call 'peter-peter-peter' is audible for hundreds of yards.
They travel in winter mixed flocks with Chickadees and Nuthatches, sharing predator alerts.
Their range has expanded dramatically north over the last century, likely due to bird feeders.
They can grip seeds in their feet while hammering them open like a woodpecker.
They were formerly called 'Tomtits' in older American field guides.
Young stay with parents for weeks longer than most songbirds, sometimes helping at the next nest.
The tuft isn't decorative — it raises and lowers as a mood signal to flock-mates.
They'll mob owls and hawks as a group, driving them out of the territory.
Downy Woodpecker: They're the smallest woodpecker in North America — barely larger than a sparrow.
Their tongue wraps around the inside of their skull when retracted — works as a shock absorber.
They drum on hollow branches to communicate, not to find food — it's their version of a song.
Males have a red patch on the nape; females don't — easiest way to tell them apart.
They can hammer up to 20 times per second without giving themselves a headache.
They're nearly identical to Hairy Woodpeckers except in size — a common ID challenge.
Their stiff tail feathers act as a third leg while climbing vertical bark.
They sleep in cavities they excavate themselves, often a different one each night.
Pairs stay bonded year-round, foraging in loose association outside nesting season.
They'll take peanut butter, suet, and sunflower seeds — one of the easiest woodpeckers to attract.
White-throated Sparrow: There are two color morphs — white-striped and tan-striped — and they almost always mate with the opposite morph.
Their song sounds like 'Oh sweet Canada Canada Canada' — or 'Old Sam Peabody,' depending who you ask.
White-striped birds are more aggressive; tan-striped birds are more nurturing parents.
Canadian researchers recently discovered their song has been shifting pitch over decades.
They migrate at night, navigating by the stars.
They scratch through leaf litter with both feet at once, a hop-and-kick move.
Each fall they put on 40% of their body weight in fat to fuel migration.
They roost communally in winter in dense thickets, up to 50 birds together.
Males arrive at breeding grounds a full two weeks before females.
Their distinctive yellow 'eyebrow' between bill and eye is often missed on quick looks.
Northern Mockingbird: A single male's repertoire can include over 200 distinct songs, many stolen from other species.
They learn new songs throughout their entire lives, unlike most songbirds.
They sing at night under streetlights — unmated males most of all.
They mimic not just birds but also car alarms, cell phone ringtones, and dog barks.
Their scientific name, Mimus polyglottos, means 'many-tongued mimic.'
They aggressively attack hawks, cats, and humans who come near their nests.
They remember individual human faces and will target people who've bothered their nest before.
They're the state bird of five US states.
A male that can't find a mate will sing through the entire night for weeks.
Their population has expanded northward with urbanization — they love suburban yards.
Brown-headed Cowbird: They're brood parasites — they never build nests, instead laying eggs in other species' nests.
A single female can lay up to 40 eggs per year, spread across dozens of host nests.
Their eggs often hatch earlier than the host's, giving the cowbird chick a fatal size advantage.
They originally followed bison herds across the Great Plains, eating insects the herds stirred up.
Modern cattle replaced the bison, letting cowbirds expand across the entire continent.
They've been documented parasitizing over 220 different host species.
Some host species now recognize and reject cowbird eggs — a visible evolutionary arms race.
Adult cowbirds have no instinct for how to raise young — they learn species identity from foster parents.
Despite that, young cowbirds still grow up to find and mate with other cowbirds.
They're a leading cause of decline for some songbird species they parasitize.
White-breasted Nuthatch: They're one of the only birds that regularly walks DOWN tree trunks head-first.
They cache seeds in bark crevices, often wedging a shell between two pieces of bark to hammer open.
Their name 'nuthatch' comes from 'nut-hacker' — how they open hard seeds.
Males feed females during courtship as a display of foraging skill.
They mate for life and defend the same territory year after year.
Their nasal 'yank-yank' call sounds like a tiny tin horn.
They join winter mixed flocks but always assert dominance over smaller Chickadees.
Their long hind toe helps them grip bark from any angle.
They line their nest cavity with animal fur, often from roadkill they find.
They've been observed 'bill-sweeping' insects around the nest entrance, possibly to repel predators.
House Sparrow: They're not sparrows in the American sense — they're Old World weaver finches introduced in 1851.
They're one of the most widely distributed wild bird species on Earth.
They were released in Brooklyn to control caterpillars; they didn't, but they spread anyway.
They nest in almost any cavity, including streetlight housings and store signage.
Males sport a black throat 'bib' — the bigger the bib, the higher his status.
They can digest grain too tough for most songbirds thanks to a muscular gizzard.
In urban areas, they've evolved slightly paler plumage to match concrete habitats.
They bathe in dust as well as water to control feather mites.
Their populations have declined sharply in parts of Europe for unknown reasons.
They take milk bottle caps as nest lining when available.
American Robin: They're not actually robins — English settlers named them after the unrelated European Robin.
They hear earthworms underground through substrate vibrations, not sight alone.
Their bright red breast comes from carotenoids, like cardinals and flamingos.
They were nearly wiped out in the DDT era — a poster species for environmental recovery.
They can produce up to three broods in a single breeding season.
In winter, flocks can number in the hundreds of thousands feeding on fruit.
Their nests often include mud collected from puddles, molded with their breasts.
Young robins have speckled breasts — the solid red appears after their first molt.
They're the state bird of Connecticut, Michigan, and Wisconsin.
A robin can live over 13 years in the wild if it survives its first dangerous year.
White-crowned Sparrow: Their striking black-and-white head stripes don't appear until their second year.
They migrate up to 2,600 miles between Alaskan breeding grounds and Mexican wintering sites.
Each dialect region has its own song — Pacific coast birds can't understand Rocky Mountain birds.
They were used in a famous experiment that discovered how migratory birds navigate by stars.
Some populations can go without sleep for two weeks during migration.
Males learn their song in the first 100 days of life and never change it.
They favor hedge rows and brushy edges — tidy suburbs are poor habitat.
Their winter flocks have strict pecking orders based on age and sex.
Their genome has helped scientists understand how jet lag affects the human body.
They're one of the most studied songbirds in neuroscience due to their predictable song learning.
Red-winged Blackbird: Males defend territories so vigorously they attack hawks, crows, and occasionally humans.
A single marsh can hold thousands of males during breeding season, each defending a territory.
Females are brown and streaked — often mistaken for a completely different species.
Males display their scarlet shoulder patches to claim territory and hide them to avoid attack.
A successful male mates with up to 15 females in a single breeding season.
Winter flocks can number in the millions, creating black clouds visible from miles away.
They roost communally in winter with grackles, starlings, and cowbirds — flocks of 40+ million.
Their 'conk-la-ree' call is considered the classic springtime marsh sound in eastern North America.
They're one of the most abundant land birds in North America, with ~150 million individuals.
Their shoulder patches are covered by dark feathers when threatened — a visual 'dimmer switch.'
Hairy Woodpecker: They look nearly identical to Downy Woodpeckers, just larger — a classic ID challenge.
The easiest tell: a Hairy's bill is as long as its head, a Downy's is much shorter.
They tunnel deeper than Downies to extract carpenter ant larvae from wood.
Their tongue can extend 2 inches past the tip of the beak to pull insects from holes.
They drum slower and louder than Downies — like a big drummer in a small band.
They'll drum on metal — gutters, stop signs, chimney caps — to advertise farther.
They excavate a new nest cavity every spring, leaving old ones for other species.
They favor larger trees than Downies, which lets the two species coexist without competing.
They can live 14+ years in the wild if they survive their first winter.
Their red nape patch on males actually varies in size based on hormone levels.
Rock Pigeon: Every feral pigeon is descended from wild Rock Doves domesticated ~5,000 years ago.
They were brought to North America by French settlers in the 1600s.
They can find their way home from hundreds of miles away — mechanism still not fully understood.
Their milk production (crop milk) is hormonally identical to mammalian lactation.
They were used to deliver messages in both World Wars — some earned military medals.
They see ultraviolet light humans can't perceive, giving them a richer color world.
They've been trained to distinguish Monet from Picasso paintings in studies.
Their iridescent neck plumage works through microscopic feather structure, not pigment.
A single pair can produce up to 12 offspring per year in warm climates.
Charles Darwin kept pigeons and used them as his main breeding study for 'On the Origin of Species.'
Chipping Sparrow: Their name comes from the mechanical, sewing-machine-like 'chip' song that fills spring mornings.
They're one of the earliest-breeding songbirds to return north in spring.
They often line their nests with horsehair — giving them the old name 'hair bird.'
Their rust-colored cap and black eye-line are only present in breeding plumage.
Winter-plumaged birds look completely different and confuse many birders.
They prefer open grassy edges — manicured suburban lawns are surprisingly good habitat.
They feed almost exclusively on grass seeds outside the breeding season.
Males sing up to 300 times per hour during peak courtship.
Their song has been documented to speed up over the course of a single morning.
They migrate in loose flocks with Dark-eyed Juncos and occasionally other sparrow species.
Lincoln's Sparrow: Named for Thomas Lincoln, who collected the first specimen for Audubon in 1833 — no relation to the President.
They're notoriously shy — more often heard than seen.
Their buffy breast band and fine streaking distinguish them from Song and Swamp Sparrows.
They breed mostly in Canadian bog habitats and only pass through suburbs in migration.
Their song is one of the richest and most complex of any sparrow.
They're one of the most difficult birds for machine learning models to classify correctly.
They typically forage on the ground under dense cover, almost never visiting feeders.
When they do visit a feeder, they prefer millet to sunflower.
Their migration path funnels through the central US, making them rare in the far east.
Thomas Lincoln was only 21 years old when he collected the first specimen on Audubon's expedition.
Gray Catbird: They mimic over 100 other bird songs, car alarms, and frog calls — but always in a raspy 'catbird voice.'
Their signature 'mew' call sounds uncannily like a housecat.
They're related to mockingbirds and share the mimicry trait — all are in the family Mimidae.
They winter as far south as Panama but return to the same backyard patch year after year.
They cache food in bark crevices, marking spots with specific mimicked calls.
Males sing from dense cover, making them hard to spot despite being vocal.
They'll aggressively mob predators, including Cooper's Hawks and domestic cats.
They recognize and reject Brown-headed Cowbird eggs, unlike many host species.
Both parents feed young, and broods fledge in just 10–11 days.
Their skulking behavior in brush earned them the informal nickname 'black hermit.'
Carolina Wren: They're loud for their size — a single wren can fill an entire backyard with 'tea-kettle tea-kettle tea-kettle.'
Pairs mate for life and stay on their territory year-round, never migrating.
Harsh winters can devastate local populations, sometimes taking decades to recover.
They've been steadily expanding their range northward with milder winters.
Only the male sings, but both members of the pair call back and forth constantly.
They nest in almost anything — hanging baskets, mailboxes, discarded boots, rolled-up newspapers.
They pump their tail up and down while foraging, as if counting rhythm.
Their song's rolling 'tea-kettle' pattern can be delivered over 3,000 times in a single day.
They glean insects from bark using their slightly down-curved bill.
They're the official state bird of South Carolina.
European Starling: All 200+ million North American Starlings descend from about 60 birds released in Central Park in 1890.
They were released by a Shakespeare enthusiast who wanted all the birds in his plays to live in America.
They're extraordinary mimics — they can imitate car alarms, human speech, and other birds.
Large flocks — called murmurations — move in hypnotic synchronized patterns against the sky.
Each bird in a murmuration tracks only its seven nearest neighbors to stay coordinated.
They compete aggressively with native cavity-nesters like Bluebirds and Woodpeckers.
Their plumage appears black but is actually iridescent green and purple in direct light.
Winter plumage is heavily spotted; breeding plumage is glossy black with a yellow bill.
They've learned to steal milk from cows when farmers aren't looking.
Mozart kept a pet Starling that could sing portions of his compositions.
American Goldfinch: They're one of the strictest vegetarians among North American birds — almost purely seed-eaters.
Their bright yellow breeding plumage is replaced by drab olive in winter.
They're one of the latest nesters in North America — often not until late July.
They time breeding for thistle seed abundance, which they feed to chicks.
Because of their late nesting, Cowbirds rarely parasitize them — the cowbird chicks starve on the seed diet.
Their bounding, roller-coaster flight pattern is accompanied by 'per-chick-o-ree' calls.
Males perform looping aerial displays to impress females.
They'll gather in 'winter finch irruptions' if their food crop fails up north.
They drink and bathe extensively — more water-dependent than most seed-eating songbirds.
They're the state bird of New Jersey, Iowa, and Washington.
Blue Jay: Their 'blue' color is an optical illusion — feather structure scatters blue light, the pigments are brown.
They plant oak forests: they carry acorns miles and cache more than they ever retrieve.
They're one of the few birds that actively mob Red-tailed Hawks.
They can perfectly mimic Red-shouldered and Red-tailed Hawk calls, scaring away smaller birds.
A jay can hold 2–3 acorns in its expandable throat and one more in its bill.
Family groups stay together for up to two years, with older siblings helping raise younger ones.
They have complex social hierarchies and recognize each other as individuals.
They 'anting' behavior — rubbing ants on their feathers — may help deter parasites.
Their crest position signals mood: raised for excitement or aggression, flat for calm.
Their scientific name Cyanocitta cristata means 'crested blue chatterer.'
American Crow: They can recognize individual human faces and pass grudges down through generations.
Scientists in Seattle documented crows targeting specific people years after a single bad encounter.
They use tools — bending twigs into hooks, dropping walnuts on roads for cars to crack.
Family groups stay together up to five years, with siblings helping raise the next brood.
A 'roost' of crows in winter can number in the tens of thousands.
They hold what look like 'funerals' around dead flock members — likely learning about threats.
Their intelligence rivals that of great apes in many problem-solving tests.
They've learned to drop hard-shelled food onto asphalt to crack it using human cars.
They can count to at least four and distinguish quantities reliably.
Their population in North America has climbed past 30 million despite historic persecution.
Pine Warbler: They're the only warbler whose diet is primarily seeds — most warblers eat insects exclusively.
They'll readily visit feeders for suet and sunflower chips, unlike any other warbler.
They breed almost exclusively in pine forests — one of the few habitat-specific warblers.
Their trilled song is easily confused with Chipping Sparrow and Dark-eyed Junco.
They're one of the earliest warblers to return in spring, often arriving in March.
They're one of the few warblers that winter north of the Gulf states.
Their yellow undertail coverts (unlike Yellow-rumped's) help separate them from other species.
They creep on tree trunks like small woodpeckers, probing bark crevices.
Males sing from the tops of mature pines, their songs audible a quarter mile away.
Their population has grown along with pine plantation expansion in the Southeast.
Red-bellied Woodpecker: Despite their name, the 'red belly' is barely visible — a subtle pink wash on the lower belly.
Their striking zebra-striped back and crimson cap are their real field marks.
They can extend their tongue nearly 2 inches beyond their bill to extract insects.
They've been expanding their range steadily north over the last century.
Their 'churr' call is one of the most distinctive sounds of eastern woodlands.
They cache acorns and nuts in bark crevices like Nuthatches do.
Pairs drum to each other in duets during courtship.
They occasionally catch small reptiles and tree frogs — not just insects.
They'll visit suet feeders year-round, one of the most reliable woodpecker visitors.
Young birds lack the red cap and can look like entirely different species.
Hermit Thrush: Their flute-like song is considered one of the most beautiful of any North American bird.
They're one of the few thrushes that winter in the US rather than migrating to the tropics.
They flick their wings and slowly raise their tail — a behavioral signature.
They're the state bird of Vermont.
Their song has inspired composers including Amy Beach and John Luther Adams.
They breed in deep conifer forests, often at high elevations.
They eat mostly insects in summer and fruit in winter — a big dietary shift.
They forage on the ground, flipping leaves with their bill.
Their reddish tail (contrasting with brown body) is the easiest ID feature.
They're one of the latest songbirds to leave the breeding grounds in fall.
Common Grackle: Their 'song' is often compared to a rusty gate — mechanical and grating.
They've been expanding their range northward, reaching much of Canada.
They're highly gregarious in winter — flocks can number in the millions.
Their long tail has a distinctive keel shape when flying, unlike any other backyard blackbird.
Their iridescent plumage shifts from bronze to purple depending on the angle.
They'll soak hard bread in water before eating it — a form of simple tool use.
They're omnivorous: seeds, insects, eggs of other birds, small fish, even mice.
Their bright yellow eye is unusual in North American blackbirds.
They've been seen sunbathing with wings spread, possibly to control feather parasites.
They form mass roosts with starlings, red-wings, and cowbirds — up to 40 million birds in winter.
Fox Sparrow: They're the heaviest North American sparrow — nearly twice the weight of a Song Sparrow.
Their rusty-red color gave them their 'fox' name.
They scratch through leaf litter with both feet in a distinctive hop-kick motion.
There are four regional color morphs once considered separate species.
They favor dense brush and rarely come into open feeders.
Their song is considered one of the finest of any sparrow — rich, whistled phrases.
They winter in thickets and often travel alone rather than in flocks.
They're an elusive migrant through much of the US — passing through quickly.
Their color varies by region: Eastern ones rusty, Alaskan ones nearly black.
They're named for the reddish chestnut color of foxes — not because they act foxy.
Eastern Phoebe: They return to breeding grounds earlier than any other flycatcher — sometimes in February.
They often build nests on human structures — eaves, bridges, barn beams.
Their tail-pumping habit is a key ID feature — they can't sit still.
They were the first banded bird in North America — John James Audubon banded one in 1804.
They return to the same nest site year after year, sometimes for decades.
Their simple 'fee-bee' song is onomatopoeic — their name comes from the song.
They eat almost exclusively flying insects, catching them in mid-air.
A single phoebe can eat hundreds of mosquitoes per day.
They build mud-and-moss nests with elaborate lichen camouflage on the outside.
They're one of the few flycatchers that will visit a cup feeder for mealworms.
Brown Thrasher: Their song repertoire is the largest of any North American bird — over 1,000 distinct phrases.
They're related to mockingbirds and catbirds — all mimics in family Mimidae.
Their long curved bill is used for sweeping leaf litter aside, not for probing.
They mimic other birds but with less faithful reproduction than mockingbirds.
They're shy despite being large, usually hiding in thick cover.
They're aggressive nest defenders — they'll attack snakes, raccoons, and occasionally humans.
They're the state bird of Georgia.
Their yellow eye is a distinctive feature, separating them from the similar Wood Thrush.
Young birds have gray eyes that turn yellow as they mature.
Like other thrashers, they're mostly ground-dwellers despite being songbirds.
Cedar Waxwing: Their crest and black mask make them one of the most distinctive-looking backyard birds.
Their yellow tail tips come from pigments in the fruit they eat — natural tattoos.
Some tails now appear orange instead of yellow due to pigments from introduced honeysuckle.
They pass berries to each other in line — a courtship behavior unique to waxwings.
They can become intoxicated eating fermented berries and crash into windows.
They have almost no song — only high, thin whistles.
They breed later than almost any other songbird — timed to summer fruit.
They're highly social — always seen in flocks, never alone.
The red 'wax' spots on their wings are pigmented keratin extensions of the feathers.
Their flocks can strip an entire crabapple tree of fruit in minutes.
Field Sparrow: Their song accelerates like a bouncing ball settling — one of the most distinctive sparrow songs.
They favor shrubby old fields — mature forests and manicured lawns both drive them out.
They're declining across much of their range as farmland is abandoned or developed.
Their pink bill is a key field mark — most other sparrows have dark bills.
They're among the earliest songbirds to begin singing in spring.
Their nests are often in thorny bushes, offering natural protection from predators.
They'll raise 2–3 broods per summer if conditions are good.
They're one of the sparrows most affected by agricultural intensification.
They often occur alongside Savannah Sparrows but prefer slightly brushier habitat.
Their song is used by many bird-song learning apps as a reference trainer.
Ruby-crowned Kinglet: They're one of North America's smallest birds — lighter than a quarter.
The male's ruby crown only shows when he's excited or displaying.
They feed almost constantly, consuming insects equivalent to many times their body weight daily.
They hover-glean insects from branch tips, wings beating almost hummingbird-fast.
They breed far north in boreal forests and only pass through most of the US on migration.
They can survive temperatures down to -40°F by fluffing and roosting communally.
Their song is astonishing for such a tiny bird — loud, rich, and varied.
They often travel with Golden-crowned Kinglets in mixed migration flocks.
Their tiny legs are so weak they can't walk — they can only hop.
They constantly flick their wings while foraging — a restless, distinctive behavior.
Savannah Sparrow: Named for Savannah, Georgia, where the first specimen was collected — not for its habitat.
They're one of the most abundant grassland sparrows in North America.
Their insect-like 'tsip-tsip-tsip-seeeee' song is easily missed or confused with crickets.
They show a yellow wash in front of the eye — a subtle but reliable ID mark.
There are over a dozen regional subspecies, including the endangered 'Ipswich' Sparrow.
They migrate at night, using the stars for navigation.
They favor open fields and almost never come to backyard feeders.
A single male's song can be delivered up to 600 times per hour during peak breeding.
They run along the ground rather than hopping — unusual for a sparrow.
Their populations have declined as grasslands have been converted to suburbs.
Rose-breasted Grosbeak: Their massive pink beak is adapted for crushing large seeds and fruit.
Males sing from inside the nest while incubating — rare behavior among songbirds.
Their song sounds like 'a robin that has taken singing lessons.'
Males have a striking rose-red triangle on the breast; females are streaky brown.
They migrate up to 3,000 miles each spring to breed in deciduous forests.
They winter in Central and South America, often visiting coffee plantations.
They hybridize with Black-headed Grosbeaks where their ranges overlap in the Great Plains.
Their scientific name is Pheucticus ludovicianus — 'Louisiana painter.'
They'll readily visit sunflower feeders during spring migration.
Males can live over 12 years in the wild — long for a migratory songbird.
unidentified: This entry holds detections that didn't lock onto a species — small mysteries awaiting review.
Often these are partial views, distant birds, or birds the classifier hadn't seen enough of to guess.
Sometimes clicking through them reveals first-of-year sightings hidden in plain sight.
They represent the frontier of the observatory — the unknown still in the yard.
Reviewing them is one of the most valuable ways to improve the model.
Some will be genuinely unidentifiable even to experts — blurry, too distant, partially hidden.
Others are species the model has never seen and can't name yet.
A high unidentified count after a storm often means migrants are passing through.
Flagging these for manual review is how rare species get added to the yard list.
The more species the model learns, the fewer unidentifieds it produces.
American Tree Sparrow: Despite the name, they rarely nest in trees — they breed on the Arctic tundra.
They winter as far south as the northern US and head back north to breed.
They can eat snow as their only water source in winter.
Their central breast spot distinguishes them from similar-looking Chipping Sparrows.
They form winter flocks of 30–50 birds, often mixed with juncos.
They need to eat approximately 30% of their body weight in food per day in winter.
Their bicolored bill — dark upper, yellow lower — is a reliable field mark.
They arrive at winter feeders in waves, often synchronized with cold fronts.
They breed within the Arctic Circle, where daylight continues nearly 24 hours a day.
Their population has declined by about 50% since 1966, possibly due to climate shifts in the tundra.
Northern Flicker: They're one of the few woodpeckers that feeds on the ground — ants are their favorite food.
A single flicker can eat over 5,000 ants in a day.
Eastern birds have yellow wing and tail linings; Western birds have red — once considered separate species.
Their 'wicka-wicka-wicka' call carries for hundreds of yards.
They drum on metal objects to advertise — gutters, chimney caps, aluminum siding.
They're one of the largest woodpeckers to visit feeders, especially in winter.
Their stiff tail feathers brace them against trees while excavating.
Males have a black 'mustache' mark; females lack it.
They're cavity nesters and often reuse old nest holes for multiple years.
Their population has declined steadily, possibly due to competition from European Starlings.
Red-breasted Nuthatch: They smear pine pitch around their nest entrance, possibly to deter predators.
They're smaller and more boldly marked than White-breasted Nuthatches.
Their call sounds like a tin horn or a toy trumpet — much higher than White-breasteds.
They breed in conifer forests — suburbs only see them during irruption years.
Irruption years occur when cone crops fail in the far north, pushing birds south.
They cache seeds but often forget them — helping reforest pine habitats.
They walk down tree trunks headfirst, like White-breasted Nuthatches.
Their black eye-line makes them look permanently serious.
They'll readily visit feeders during irruptions and become unusually tame.
Their tongue can extend beyond the bill to extract insects from bark.
Pileated Woodpecker: They're the largest woodpecker in North America, crow-sized with a flaming red crest.
Their rectangular excavations in trees are unmistakable — often a foot deep.
Woody Woodpecker was inspired by them, though with an Acorn Woodpecker's call.
They favor large mature forests with plenty of dead wood for foraging.
Their deep, ringing call — 'wuk-wuk-wuk' — carries up to half a mile through forest.
They eat carpenter ants almost exclusively, which they extract with their long barbed tongue.
They're a 'keystone' species — their abandoned cavities shelter many other animals.
They were nearly extirpated in the early 1900s but have recovered remarkably.
A pair defends up to 200 acres as territory — enormous for a bird.
They can drum louder than any other North American woodpecker, audible a mile away.
Pine Siskin: They're nomads — winter ranges shift dramatically based on conifer seed crops.
In irruption years, they may appear far south in huge flocks.
Their streaky brown plumage has subtle yellow wing flashes.
They cling to feeders upside down, especially for nyjer seed.
They're highly social — always in flocks, often mixed with goldfinches.
Their flight call is a rising 'zzreeeeee' that sounds like electrical static.
They can handle the cold thanks to a high resting metabolism.
They can pack seeds into their esophagus to digest overnight when roosting.
Their populations have declined sharply in the last 50 years, reason unclear.
They occasionally hybridize with American Goldfinches where the two overlap.
Yellow-bellied Sapsucker: They drill orderly rows of small holes in tree bark to drink sap — their 'wells.'
Other species including hummingbirds and warblers visit sapsucker wells.
Their drumming pattern is irregular — a few quick taps, a pause, then more.
They migrate through most of the US to breed in Canada.
Their 'yellow belly' is subtle and often missed in field views.
They're the only truly migratory woodpecker in North America.
Males have a red throat; females have white.
Their feeding wells can seriously stress or kill young trees if too numerous.
They eat insects too — especially ants attracted to the oozing sap.
Their scientific name, Sphyrapicus varius, means 'varied hammer-striker.'
Brown Creeper: They spiral up tree trunks, always heading up — when they reach the top, they fly to the base of the next tree.
Their camouflage is so good they're often missed even when close.
They nest behind loose bark slabs — one of only a handful of songbirds that do.
Their thin curved bill probes bark crevices for insects and spider eggs.
Their song is so high-pitched many older birders can no longer hear it.
They're often the first bird other species follow in winter mixed flocks.
Their stiff pointed tail feathers prop them against the tree like a woodpecker's.
They're one of the few songbirds that hasn't been found to sing dialects.
They're secretive enough that many yard birders have them pass through unnoticed.
The European Treecreeper is their nearly identical Old World counterpart.
Yellow-rumped Warbler: They're one of the most abundant warblers in North America, with ~130 million individuals.
Their yellow rump patch — called the 'butter butt' — is diagnostic.
They're the only warbler that can digest the waxy berries of bayberry and wax myrtle.
This lets them winter much farther north than most other warblers.
They were formerly split into two species: Audubon's (west) and Myrtle (east).
In fall, breeding plumage is replaced by drab brown with yellow rump still visible.
They migrate in huge flocks, sometimes hundreds in a single tree.
They'll visit suet feeders in winter — one of the few warblers that will.
Males arrive on breeding territories two weeks before females.
Their 'chip' call is one of the most common sounds of fall migration.
Purple Finch: They're not actually purple — males are a raspberry-red, females streaky brown.
They look similar to House Finches but with cleaner markings and bulkier bill.
Roger Tory Peterson called a male 'a sparrow dipped in raspberry juice.'
Their population has declined sharply since House Finches spread east in the 1940s.
They breed in northern conifer forests and only visit most yards during winter irruptions.
They're the state bird of New Hampshire.
Their song is a liquid warble, different from House Finch's more halting song.
Their undertail coverts are unmarked — a way to separate them from House Finches.
They eat tree buds, seeds, and some fruit — less feeder-dependent than House Finches.
Females have a strong face pattern with a white eyebrow — House Finch females don't.
Eastern Bluebird: Their brilliant blue comes from feather structure, not pigment — they're actually brown underneath.
They were near extinction in the mid-1900s due to House Sparrow competition for nest cavities.
A dedicated nest-box campaign starting in the 1970s has brought them roaring back.
They're the state bird of Missouri and New York.
Pairs often raise three broods per summer if conditions are good.
Males arrive early in spring and claim nest cavities before females arrive.
They eat mostly insects in summer and fruit in winter — a big dietary shift.
They drop from perches to catch insects on the ground — a distinctive foraging style.
Young birds lack the blue color until their first molt.
They have been shown to track insect abundance and move nests mid-season if needed.
